speaker: interviewee
text: The permit to travel to Vilna from [? Tremenitz, ?] which is still within the Russian-occupied territory. And he got to Vilna, or Vilnius, as the Lithuanians call it. And he found out that there was the Japanese Ambassador was still there and giving out visas to Japan.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he and a group of the [INAUDIBLE] Hasidim from-- that were in Vilna, somehow he got one of the visas. And I understand there was a trainload of 2,000 Jews that went from Vilna directly to-- through Russia, through the Trans-Siberian Railroad into Vladivostok and Yokohama. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And they went to Japan?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to Japan. And he wound up in Kobe, K-O-B-E, in Japan. But first he stayed in Lithuania for a while. And something else happened to us. After he was gone. And so, we left my parents, my brother Sam and his fiancee, Shaindl, and my sister and I. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --suddenly the Russians decided-- or Stalin decided that we should become Russian citizens because we now live in Russia. And-- and my father all of a sudden said, no. We don't want to become Russian citizens. The war is going to be over soon. We'll go back home to Warsaw. We are Polish citizens. We don't want to become Russian citizens. Well then, if you don't want to be Russian citizens, that means you're against us. And therefore, you're an enemy of the state. Because his famous motto was you're either with us or against us, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So someone came  to the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: And as usual, they always do things on-- at-- at Midnight. One o'clock in the morning, somebody knocked on the door. And he says, come with us. And they were-- fortunately, they were nice enough to let us take our belongings. So we spread our sheets on the floor. Put everything, quickly.

speaker: interviewer
text: It had to be done in a hurry. So we put all in sheets and made big bundles. And we all took bundles. And where we're going? We don't tell you. You know, you know-- just-- just hurry up and-- and come. This was when?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was in-- ah, let's see, September 1940. Just about a year after we lived there. And we lived quite happily. Everything was fine. And they took us, by truck, to the train station. And there, they had a whole trainload of cattle cars, and a whole shalon, as they call it, S-H-A-L-O-N, shalon. I guess that's the Russian name of a transport. And they opened up the-- the cars. And they dumped us into these cattle trains, which were already prepared with bunks built like two tiers of bunks on each side of the-- of the train. And where we're going, nobody had any answers. We just got in, and they had like a pre-fabbed toilet sticking out on the-- with the exit to the-- through the door. And sure enough, they locked the doors. And by this-- this time, the townspeople came out. You know, that are our friends, the Jewish people, heard about it. And they all came to the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was only Jews on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Jews. As far as I know, there were only Jews. The-- you know, the refugees. Well, the-- well, the-- the word was [RUSSIAN]. That's the Russian version of refugees. And obviously, the others did the same thing. They didn't want to become Russian citizens. And therefore, they became, also, enemies of the state.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a term for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if it's refuseniks-- no. That-- that would be a later version of it. But no, there was-- I don't know of any other terms like that. But the townspeople, the Jewish people who we befriended and were very happy that we worked together, cried. And they were citizens because they had no choice. They lived there so they-- once they overran that part of the country, they became citizens. We were [RUSSIAN], or refugees. So we stuck to our version of being Polish, and we're going to go home as soon as the war is over. We thought the war  would be over in a couple of months or whatever. So anyway, they remained in town, and we were shipped out. As we found out later, it was Siberia. Took us over two weeks to travel in the trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there food and water?

speaker: interviewee
text: They, once a day, they would bring in a cauldron of soup, kasha. You know, that's a-- I don't know what the-- and-- and I think some bread, also. And the train would not open up. Way, way into the-- further, and deep into the country. And the-- once we were, I think, past the Ural mountains, which is Northern Russia, they started opening up the-- the trains, because we had no way of running anyway. And nobody told us where we're going. And I remember they would-- that-- that-- that toilet that they created for it got so-- so bad that my father and the other families who were on the train decided-- we threw it out. But-- but at that point, they allowed us, to you know, every time they made a stop, we would all run to the sides of the train, to the woods or whatever there was, to relieve ourselves. So-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people in a car?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, there was our family. And there was-- I think there were four families in it. It wasn't overcrowded like you-- you heard of the-- the concentration camp type or the-- it was this same, cattle cars. But it was pre-fabbed to have bunks to-- for sleeping, because it was a long distance. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the families' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm-- I was just trying to think if I could remember them. But I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: On the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. On the train, well, we traveled. It wasn't that much. I know we were laid, mostly, on the bunks. There wasn't that much room to move around. And when they opened the trains later on, we would run out, of course, and get fresh air. And one task that I had was to run across all the wagons and pick up-- you know, they had those lanterns that were lit with candles. And I would go and collect all those candles from the lanterns so that my mother also would have enough candles for Shabbos. And I collected the wax and the partial candles. And then, later on, after I had a whole bagful, I would melt it down and make small candles for my mother to have for Shabbos. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was on the train or in Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was on the train. I mean, later on and after we got off the train, we used all the wax in the-- the candles for-- for that. And we traveled for, like I say, over two weeks. Until we finally came to a city named Novosibirsk. That's N-O-V-O-S-I-B-I-R-S-K, Novosibirsk. That's the-- the main city, or the capital city of the-- of Siberia. From there, we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the weather there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time was still not too bad. It was already winter setting in. Because the winter starts pretty early up there. And we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --we got transferred from those wagons, from the cattle cars, to a smaller train that took us-- by that time, we still didn't know where we were going, and why we were going. Nobody-- there was no-- it was just like a bunch of people being led, but with no leader. And so, they transferred us to another train, which went to a city of Tomsk, which is much further north from Novosibirsk, T-O-M-S-K, Tomsk. And from Tomsk, they took us on a narrow-gauge train that went to a-- a small town of Asino, A-S-I-N-O. And from there, still further north. We're talking about 200 miles north of Novosibirsk, which is already Siberia. This is going into the tundras, into the forests of-- of Siberia. And from Asino to [? Beregovo, ?] a small village. Now that, already, was a prison village. [? Beregovo, ?] B-E-R-E-G-A-V-O, [? Beregovo. ?] That was a prison, a gulag, as you've probably heard the name. And from there--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they didn't stop there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't stop there. We got put on little flat cars. And we went from there to another place, which was called the Tajga. T-A-J-G-A, Tajga, the Russian spelling. And from there on, from-- from Asino, all the people that lived there were originally prisoners of the Soviet Union. The-- there were White Russians that were sent to Siberia by Stalin. And there was no return for them either. And so that we got to the Tajga at night. And they dumped us off from those flat cars. And it was already snowing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have coats, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had coats. We-- we-- we had all-- most of our belongings, thank God. Because that-- that made us-- helped us to survive over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we-- we lay down, right off the tracks, and it started snowing. So we covered ourself with blankets. And we found out that the next morning, when we woke up, we were actually on a cemetery. They were just-- We were wondering why there was bumps, and that there were the, what do you call it? The graves-- The tomb--

speaker: interviewee
text: --of the previous prisoners that were brought over there. It was a prison camp. And we were dumped over there. And the next morning, we got up. And we're in the middle of nowhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: There's no-- and in the distance, we saw some barracks. And we all started you know, crying and screaming, and worrying what we wanted-- you want me to stop? Nm-mm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. At that time, we knew what-- oh, the-- the-- I guess the [NON-ENGLISH], or the commander of the prison, came out and says, forget Warsaw. Forget Poland. This is your home. This where you're going to live. And this is where you're going to die. There's no way out of here. And the-- the word that they used in Russian is [RUSSIAN]. That means you-- you'll get used to it or you'll drop dead, one of the two. So there's no-- no choice. No--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- in desperation, we literally revolted.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Not only us, I mean, the whole trainload. The whole group. How many people were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was quite a few hundred people. I would say, at last 200 people on-- on that gulag, on that camp. And so we loaded all our belongings on the flatbeds. They were still there. The-- the locomotive wasn't there. But we started pushing the trains by hand, go back to the previous town of [? Beregovo. ?] And we were stopped by the Russian military. I don't know if they were police or military.

speaker: interviewer
text: Different people than in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I mean, they were in uniform. And especially one guy was on a horse. And they had a machine gun set up. They said, you guys better go back or we're going to shoot. And we said, you may as well shoot us. We're not going to go back. I don't know why we became brave. But I mean, it was desperation. And this guy on the horse tried to grab my sister, and to get her on the horse. And we pulled her away, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister. And they-- and there was a hassle. I mean, literally, a hand-to-hand battle. And they said, you know, we can't talk to everybody at the same time. They says, why don't you get a committee together? I mean, at that time, I didn't know what they were talking about. But my father, [? on chance, ?] spoke Russian quite well because he was in the Russian army before the-- in the First World War. And that was-- he was conscripted when the Russians occupied Poland. And he had spent four years in the Russian army, including Yakutsk, which is part of Siberia also. So he was able to communicate with them. So he was chosen to be a representative of our people, And including another. I think they wanted like 10 people to be a delegation. And they brought in a whole bunch of high officers by helicopter, I think, or whatever, to-- And I don't recall the whole thing. It's really muddy in my mind. But all I know is that they said through my father and the people, they says, go back. We'll-- we'll-- once you settle down, we'll try to make arrangements to improve the situation. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But you have no choice. There's no going back from-- from here, going back to Novosibirsk, or to Poland, or to anywhere else. And so they convinced them to-- because my father said there is no-- no alternative. We better go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So nobody was shot or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody was shot yet. Not yet. And so we got back on the-- on the platforms. And the locomotive, this time, pushed us back into camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we got into the barracks, which are tremendous, open, big, wooden barracks. And the-- the things that they agreed to at the time, supposedly, was that they're going to subdivide the barracks for family units. And they asked who can build little stoves, out of-- brick stoves, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there's one of the people that was on our train. He says he's a bricklayer. You know. So he gave him a job too. And they did subdivide the barracks in-- in smaller units. And each family got a unit. In the meantime, they came back. They-- they took the delegation, the representatives, my father and nine more men, 10 men. And they took them away. They were-- said, you're the instigators. And you're the leaders. And they took them to prison. To prison. We're already in Siberia, but they had a better prison somewhere else. So they took the 10 men, including my father, and took them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: When is this now?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is right after we came there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, what time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in about, oh-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] November, 1940, or so. It was already winter up there. And they held him for about four months. And in the meantime, I remember the-- there came a-- a-- a-- a Jewish person came back to camp. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: From the group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not from this group. Just a-- an informer, and he was in Jewish garb, if you believe it or not. And he said, all the men were shot. And it was put up by the-- by the NKVD, or whatever. And he says, the men that were taken, they're all shot. And you better behave yourself, or else it's going to be--

speaker: interviewer
text: And he-- and he was dressed religiously?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was one of-- supposedly one of us, and one of our people. And my mother, when she heard him, said, forget it. Don't believe him. He's-- he's telling a lie. You know, she says, my husband is alive and-- But in any case, we had to settle down. And we did what they-- what they asked us to do. And that is cut down trees. My brother, Sam, together with another group of people, became what they call [NON-ENGLISH]. In other words, they-- they were able to cut down more than the norm. And because of that, they were given extra rations and better food. And I became a-- they made me a-- a-- in Russian it's a [RUSSIAN]. That means I had to mark the trees after they cut them. By the way, there's a little story how they became [NON-ENGLISH], and be able to cut down more than the norm. We had to cut the trees-- they had to cut the trees in, I think, in 10-foot length, or whatever. So they cut it 10 foot and an inch longer. Although, it's in metric. But after they-- I came through with-- I-- I would usually follow the commander. And I-- I was given tools to mark the trees you know, by size and by what kind of a tree it was designated to be, whether it's for lumber, or for rifle butts, or whatever the designation of the tree-- of the wood was. And so, I would mark the trees with the special chisels at the end of the tree. And also, I had a-- a wooden board. I would mark how-- how many trees and what they were doing. When they cut down the trees, instead of making it 10-feet long, they would make it 10 and 1 inch. And after I mark it, the end, they would cut the-- the ends off and get a new marking on it. In other words, so that they almost made like, double their-- their number. You had to use all kinds of gimmicks to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would the-- how did the Russians treat you, the guards and--

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- there are no guards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, there's no guards.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no guards. We had a commander who was in charge of the camp, and another commander who was in charge of the working crews. He, himself, was a-- was a prisoner. In other words, the whole community over there, it was all prisoners. They  couldn't leave either. But they excelled, supposedly, in-- in what they were doing. And they were given certain ranks. And for that, we would get-- we would get a-- once a day, a pot of kasha, in the soup. And I think the-- the bread was given out once a week. And-- and most of the people would eat it up, like, in one day or whatever. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As far as food is concerned, we were fortunate. As a family, and we had our belongings with us. We were able to barter with the local farmers. Although it was a collective farm, but they were able to grow their own food you know, on the side, besides the-- the collective farm. And we were allowed-- we would give a-- a pass to walk to the next-- to the other towns and barter with the-- with the people there. Even though they are prisoners too, they were the White Russians from the Stalin area, from before, yeah. And but we were able to give a-- get a bucket of potatoes, let's say, for a shirt, or a-- a jacket. Or a-- a jacket would bring a-- a chicken or something like that. And because we were able to trade--

speaker: interviewer
text: To barter--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and barter our stuff for food, there was no money in the exchange. Only things for food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Food, goods. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And bring it back, and-- and feed the family that way. It was the--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that last for? That you're able to stay-- stay--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were able to trade. And we had to walk like, 20 miles, to that-- 20 kilometers, yeah, to the next town. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had your dad come--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was able--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: What sustained us. And unfortunately, those who did not have, mainly, the-- we lost quite a few people there. We-- we lost, during the wintertime, you know, people were-- frozen parts of the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --bodies. And those who had no-- nothing to trade, unfortunately-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --literally died of hunger. And we-- sometimes we tried-- we took upon us, a couple of guys that were-- my mother would take care of, for the boys. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: She was still lighting shabbos candles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, she would not eat no no-kosher food all through the years. She was a saint. My mother was a [NON-ENGLISH]. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: She would keep shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: She would keep shabbat. And although we all would work seven days a week. Well, maybe six days. I don't know what it is. But holidays and included, had to go to the-- to the woods and work and cut down trees, except for one Yom Kippur. We refused to work. We went to the forest. We sat down and-- and did nothing. And because of that, I think, we got in trouble. I don't know why. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that year, that wintertime. That year--

speaker: interviewee
text: '41. Yeah, it was already January, February, whatever the--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got in trouble?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So. But--
